data.title: Walmart: How Tariff Panic Created A Buy Window Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
I believe market overestimated the tariffs and predicted heavier consequences, which did not materialize for most of the first quarter of the year.

Additionally, Walmart's managers were optimistic about their business, yet they shared very conservative revenue guidance for FY 2026 of 3% to 4%, that I believe will be beaten, just like last year's.

Walmart's e-commerce momentum and right strategic choices position Walmart to exceed guidance with a 28% upside. If these next earnings beat expectations, the stock is likely to gain momentum.

Less Severe Tariffs Than Feared

Walmart stock (NYSE:WMT) made new highs on February 13th this year when the news of Reciprocal tariffs was announced.

After that, the price fell 22% until April 9th, when President Trump's reciprocal tariffs were, for the most part, suspended for 90 days.

With talks of 25%, 50%, and 145% on the tariffs front, there was a lot of pessimism around. And yet, these tariffs were postponed multiple times, or quickly renegotiated afterwards if implemented.

For Walmart, this coincided with the first quarter of its 2026' Fiscal year (FY 2026 Q1), which starts on 01 February and ends on April 30.

While tariffs were all over the news and made a strong impact on the stock price, I judge that they will not have a significant effect on the company's financials.

If we cut through the noise and focus only on the tariff changes actually implemented in Q1 that could impact Walmart, this is what we find:

  • February 04, the U.S. added a 10% tariff on imports from China.
  • March 04, 25% tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico, also 20% tariff on Chinese imports.
  • March 05, one-month exemption for automakers, on the 25% tariffs impacting goods from Mexico and Canada.
  • March 06, Tariffs postponed for one month the 25% tariffs on many imports from Mexico and Canada.
  • April 02, last 30 days of the quarter, 10% baseline tariff on imports due to start April 5, and in April 9 higher rates, reciprocal tariffs, for nations that have trade surpluses with the U.S..
  • April 03, 25% tariffs on auto imports.
  • April 05, 10% tariffs on almost all countries.
  • April 09, China on a 145% tariff rate.
  • April 11, Temporary exemption for electronics.
  • April 14, tomatoes from Mexico will be on a 20.9% tariff.
  • April 29, 25% tariffs on automobiles and auto parts were eased.

Those were the first quarter tariffs that affected Walmart. To summarize, Chinese goods faced tariffs of 10% for one month, 20% another month, and one last month with 145%. Additionally, there were tariffs of 25% on auto parts for a month and 10% on goods from every other country, exempting electronics.

Around 16.7% of Walmart's total revenues comes from U.S. stores selling general merchandise, which includes Chinese goods. Another 9% comes from the Health and Wellness category, some of which comes from China.

But again, much was said, while real actions taken were the 10% and 20% tariffs for most of Q1. That doesn't mean that suddenly the American consumers will stop buying their non-stick cookware and electric scooters just because of a 5 to 20 dollars increase in price. Even though consumer sentiment is very low, total disposable income continues to increase in the U.S.

Chinese items will continue to be cheaper since competitors had little time to start producing similar items in a country with fewer tariffs imposed.

Growth Drivers and Tailwinds

Walmart's E-commerce is gaining momentum and is becoming more important for the company's total revenues. This trend helps increase their subscription model, both for Walmart and Sam's Club.

Walmart's recent acquisition of Vizio also plays a strategic role.

Vizio is a company that, besides delivering smart TVs and sound bars, offers valuable consumer data insights. This data is used by Walmart and also sold to Walmart's business partners and vendors.

This improves Walmart's advertising business, which has far better margins than margins from selling goods. It provides effective ads placements with higher conversions and better visibility for sellers, similarly to how Amazon (AMZN) uses its ads.

The company invested heavily in supply chain automation, such that 61.3% of the FY 2025' Capex was devoted to it. This will increase overall productivity and allow for better execution of their e-commerce strategy and faster deliveries, while reducing salary costs in their 164 U.S.-based distribution centers.

Even though the U.S. administration was raising tariffs, other countries continued their business normally. Currency fluctuations that have negatively affected the company last year may instead have a positive impact this year.

In FY 2024, the company reported a $3 billion gain and in FY 2025 a $3 billion loss from fluctuations in currency exchange rates.

This allows us to estimate the effect on the company's international business through the average Dollar index (DXY) price by comparing to the previous two years' average.

If the average (DXY) price of FY 2026 Q1 continues until the end of the year, I expect a $5.1 billion positive effect for the company. For FY 2026 Q1, a $1.2 billion is expected.

Projecting Revenues

The point where I mostly disagree with management is their 3% - 4% net sales growth, which is around 3% to 4% revenue growth, too. This projection is too conservative in my view.

Walmart's most important revenue source is its U.S. stores, which have been dropping in number, from 4717 in FY 2023 to 4605 in FY 2025, but increased revenues per store.

What has been working for Walmart is its initiative in e-commerce. The online business is gaining momentum and becoming more relevant for the company.

Here are the revenues separating physical stores and e-commerce, along with the management's guidance for FY 2026 estimate.

I don't think Walmart's performance here will be as weak as management guidance suggests. As you can see, the pace of e-commerce is picking up while physical stores are losing momentum. That's part of the business strategy of relying more on online sales as they are closing physical stores that are too close to one another.

This is the right strategy to pave the way for increased efficiency for the business. Even though Walmart is closing some U.S. stores, it has opened 116 stores internationally in the last fiscal year.

Accounting for the above information and for what I project will be a low tariff impact on Walmart's revenues, I estimate a 6% YoY increase, with projected FY 2026 revenue of $722.248 billion.

I was still a little bit cautious in this projection of total revenues. As you can see, all items but international e-commerce were projected lower, but the company still has a very large growth potential due to the online business. Note that this projection exceeds the upper range of analysts' estimates of $706.5 billion.

Risks

Walmart looks strong, but it's not bulletproof. The biggest risk right now is tariffs. If those suspended 145% rates on Chinese goods were to come back, Walmart's margins could take a severe hit. They will eventually shift suppliers for U.S. centers, but for other countries still viable.

For consumers, while their paychecks are still growing, consumer sentiment about the economy is very low. If unemployment increases, non-essential purchases like electronics and home goods could decrease. These are key categories for Walmart and would impact revenues.

Finally, the e-commerce bet isn't certain. There are more risks in food delivery than in selling non-perishable items, for example. Also, while Vizio's ad business is promising, it is not guaranteed. If online growth stalls or those new automated warehouses don't deliver the savings Walmart expects, the stock's 20x EV/EBITDA multiple could look very expensive.

Valuation

For this valuation, I have considered the revenue to grow at 6.1% (vs. 3-4% guide).

Cost of goods sold to continue its downward trend.

SG&A as a percentage of revenue to continue declining as Walmart increases its e-commerce penetration.

Depreciation in line with the last years.

Interest expense equal to last year's.

Income tax rate, a small increase in line with the average and the company's guidance.

Day sales outstanding, in line with the current trend.

Days inventory outstanding, in line with the current downward trend.

Days payable outstanding, in line with the current downward trend.

A CapEx of 3.25% of revenues, according to company's guidance.

And I have not considered the issuance or repayments of new debt, or equity issuance or buybacks.

With the valuation date being 05/13/2025, the long-term growth rate of 2.43%, according to the last 20 years inflation rate, and the current EV/EBITDA traded approximately 20.

The fair value per stock for Walmart (WMT) is $123.07.

Resulting in a 28% upside.

Conclusion

Although high tariffs on Chinese goods present a risk, Walmart is the largest retailer in the world by revenue and won't suffer for a long time as they have the means to relocate production and find other providers around the world if needed.

The company also has a bright future in the e-commerce. With the right mix of products and their improved ad utilization via Vizio, they can position themselves as a major threat to Amazon, being a stronger competitor for market share.

I believe we could see a rally in the stock price if FY 2026 Q1 results beat expectations. Also, due to the current low petroleum prices, could Walmart's operational costs could drop and optimism could lift.

I rated Walmart as a Buy with a fair value per stock of $123.09, indicating a 28% upside.

data.title: Serve Robotics: Don't Chase
data.description:
Serve Robotics Inc. (NASDAQ:SERV) continues to offer enormous potential, but the delivery robot business is off to a slow start. The company has a lot of promise with limited revenues to prove delivering food via robots can scale to millions in quarterly revenues. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock for now, but Serve Robotics needs a big guide for Q3 before the stock can be chased.

Rolling Out Robots

The Serve Robotics investment story is all about how quickly the company can scale the robot fleet. The company was set to take delivery of 250 robots during Q1, but the key to the story is that most of the robots were still being added to the fleet in early May.

The company reported a Daily Active Robots total for Q1 of only 73. Despite having over 300 delivery robots now, Serve Robotics only averaged less than 25% in service during Q1 '25, up just 16 robots from Q4 '24.

The whole reason the stock didn't hold $20 for a valuation of over $1.1 billion is this disconnect with the actual financials. Serve Robotics has rebounded nicely off a low below $5 on the reality of excitement again of going from 50 to 2,000 delivery robots, but the company will only take delivery of the next batch of robots in Q3 with roughly 50% of the 2,000 robots not arriving until Q4.

Serve Robotics will take weeks and months to optimize those robots to reach peak efficiency and hit the goal of $60 to $80 million in annual revenues. At the low end, Serve Robotics needs to average revenues of $30,000 per delivery robot, or $7,500 per quarter.

The food delivery company only reported Q1 Fleet revenues of $212K, up just 20% from the prior quarter. The company has the delivery robots to quadruple the DARs, but at the same revenue levels it's just ~$850K in quarterly Fleet revenues (branding ads + delivery fees).

Serve Robotics is only averaging ~$2,900 per robot per quarter. The amount works out to $11,596 per year, or only 39% of targeted amounts.

The market naturally wonders about nearly tripling revenues per robot while scaling from DARs of 73 in Q1 to a goal of 2,000 somewhere in Q1 '26. Normally in such a scenario, the productivity of each unit typically dips, not triples.

Serve Robotics has a lot of moving parts likely adding up to significant growth with the following key metrics:

  • Serving over 320,000 households, an 110% increase since December 2024.
  • Partnering with 1,500 merchants, representing a 50% increase QoQ and 5x YoY.
  • Delivery volumes up 75% from first and last weeks in Q1.

The company is clearly heading in the right direction, but the business is going to take over a year to fully scale from the 50 robots to 2,000. Not to mention, Serve Robotics is entering new markets during this process, further reducing the likely productivity levels of these new delivery robots until late 2026.

Meager Guidance

The biggest concern of shareholders has to be the meager guidance. Serve Robotics only guided to the following Q2 '25 targets:

  • Revenue expected in the range of $600k - $700k
  • Represents anticipated revenue growth of approximately 35-60% QoQ
  • Includes estimated delivery volume growth of approximately 60-75% QoQ.

The company has long discussed having the 250 new robots in Q1, yet the revenue guidance for Q2 is only $650K, at the midpoint. Serve Robotics has entered new markets in Miami and Dallas, likely leading to the limited growth because one would've expected delivery volumes to come a lot closer to the robot expansion in Q1 from 57 to start the quarter to over 300 now.

For these reasons, our research has cautioned investors about rushing into the stock. Serve Robotics now has a market cap of around $525 million with current analyst estimates of 2026 revenue of $58 million, though the current numbers appear too high considering the 1,750 new robots delivered in Q3/Q4 will need substantial time to ramp up and won't reach peak efficiency levels until far into the year.

The company has a cash balance of $191 million now and burned around $10 million in cash from operating losses. The big expense will be the new robots, so the cash balance shouldn't be included in the valuation of the stock.

When Serve Robotics reaches the $60+ million sales run rate in 2026, the stock will likely trade higher than a $525 million market cap. The company has an additional opportunity in autonomy software and to expand beyond the 2,000 robots, but the path to higher stock prices will be lumpy.

Serve Robotics likely faces another scenario where the realization of immaterial Q3 revenues holds the stock back.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that investors shouldn't chase the stock here above $9 already. Serve Robotics still has a bumpy path ahead to material revenues, making the stock a buy on dips.

data.title: CBIL:CA -- Perfectly Sensible Short-Term Money Stop For Canadians
data.description:
By their construction ye shall know them

As we know, there are thousands upon thousands of different things we can invest in. The trick is knowing which one we want to actually put our money into. One part of this is that we need to grasp just what it is that a particular instrument is trying to do. Once we've done that, we can then think through whether that matches what we're trying to do.

Yes, this is obvious, it's just horses for courses. But at least a modest understanding of how a financial instrument is constructed gives us a good guide as to whether we want to use it. Knowing this isn't positively decisive-that it matches our aim isn't enough; there might be others better. But that it does not match our aim is indeed negatively decisive. We can say that's a reasonable choice, we can also say don't do that. Positively, that is more difficult to prove.

Portfolios

A standard insistence about investing is that we need to have a portfolio. A spread across instruments and across markets so that we are not loading all our money into just one risk.

A standard insistence about saving-and note, saving is not the same as investing, not wholly the same, it isn't-is that we should have a spread as well. But while we might have a spread of risk, we also want to have a spread of liquidity. Perhaps "availability" is a more accurate word. Yes, obviously it might be a good idea to acquire a house at some point, but we do then note that if we need to get rid of the house-because we need the money to live-then it might take us some months to do that. Some months are a long time with no money. Similarly, we can gain a better income from a deposit, CD, or bond if we agree to park the money away for some years rather than be able to get it back on demand. Which is great-but the conventional wisdom is that we should also have some portion of our savings available more quickly, just in case. If that means we give up some interest income in the pursuit of sooner, or immediate, access, then so be it.

The general structure here is held to be generally true. Any arguments are more usually about how much we require in those easy access accounts, how much we stack away for the long term. Some might even argue that we should put more away long term than we really want to-it's a form of forced savings, almost as if we can't touch the money. Tax-privileged accounts where we face severe penalties for cashing out early, perhaps.

On the other hand, the idea of having a few hundred $ around-maybe even in cash-just in case we've got to call the plumber, a few thousand dollars around for "events" and perhaps $10,000 in swift access savings just in case we find ourselves between jobs. These sorts of numbers will indeed depend upon gross income and lifestyle, but the idea that we require those gradations of easy-to-access liquidity is wholly sensible. It's what we've got after those that we then invest, perhaps. As in, invest in pensions, side hustles, stocks, and all those lovely good things.

It is possible, by being judicious, to juice the returns on that money we have put aside to be liquid and sensible. Not hugely, to be sure, because being illiquid produces a higher return, therefore being liquid a lower one. But it's still possible for us to earn more than the nothing we get in plain cash by being a little sensible.

Global X 0-3 Month T-Bill ETF CAD (TSX:CBIL:CA)

This particular method is something potentially sensible for Canadians. There's no particular reason why non-Canadians would have some surplus Canadian Dollars lying around (disclosure, I have something like C$3, I was paid in C$ for doing a piece of work and didn't transfer it all), nor why they would like quick access to liquidity to a C$ savings facility. So, assume all that follows is for Canadians only.

The point of this ETF is to invest in short-term-one to three-month maturity-Canadian Treasuries. The interest is then paid out to the ETF owners/holders. And that, just about, it is.

But of course, we can go further in explaining this.

For those in the Canadian dollar, the Canadian government bonds are the zero credit risk option. It's not wholly and exactly true that governments are zero risk-ask those who lent money to the Greek one-but they are as close as the modern world is going to make available.

The tenor also matters. By agreeing to lock away our money for a longer period of time, we can gain a higher interest rate. 30-year bonds pay a higher rate than 30-day notes. 12-month CDs pay more than an at-demand deposit.

On the other hand, longer time spans come with interest rate risk. If interest rates change, then the capital value of any time-limited bond changes. The longer the time period, the greater the capital value change - that's just how it works. This also works both ways; falling interest rates increase the value of bonds, while rising rates reduce them.

The low-risk option

The low-risk option is therefore to be in government bonds with a short tenor. We have near zero credit risk and very little interest rate risk. So, such a bond is near always going to trade just around par.

Liquidity

It is possible to buy government bonds-and bills, notes-retail. The deal size is, in most jurisdictions, around the $1,000 level. So, we can definitely do this sort of investing ourselves, directly. On the other hand, if we're parking that close to cash money over time, this becomes very boring, continually collecting redemptions and then reinvesting. Actually, we'd have to be doing this with a very large amount of money for it to be worth our time. But we probably shouldn't be trading this liquidity for a return with a very large amount of money.

So, using a fund to do this paperwork for us makes sense. This also brings in even greater liquidity. A fund-like this ETF-is saleable today at something very close to net asset value or par ("very close" here means basis points, tenths of a percent), and the money will be in our account in two days (T+1 is the usual settlement date for ETFs).

So, CBIL:CA

The fund does this:

By investing in short-term Government of Canada T-Bills, backed by the "full faith and credit" of the Canadian Government, CBIL provides an interest-bearing alternative to Canadian dollar cash holdings, with potentially lower credit and rate risk compared to other fixed-income options.

Basically, the ETF provides all the paperwork for those continual rollovers of 30- to 90-days government bonds.

Because the investments are so short-term, the capital value of the fund moves with the dividend about to be paid and pretty much nothing else:

As we can see, the monthly price movements used to be higher. We should also be wary of that yield as calculated there. For this reason:

Bank of Canada base rates have come down; therefore, calculating the current yield on the past 12 months doesn't quite work when we have a monthly dividend being paid. The current yield is more like 2.6% rather than what is recorded:

That's the effect of recording a falling yield over 12 months. On the other hand, we also see that changes in interest rates don't change the capital value very much from that stock price chart up above. Yes, the yield has followed the base rate down by the continual turnover in the underlying means that capital value has stayed (almost) constant.

This is just what the trade-off is about tenor in bonds. The immediate change in coupon as base rates change is exactly what preserves capital.

Finances

The fund is invested in:

That's not, to be honest, wholly interesting since there aren't going to be that many issues that are all 30 to 90 days. But there we are.

The other finances are:

Given that daily trading, that's really a pretty liquid fund. We'd be able to sell pretty much whenever we wanted. It's a fair-sized fund too, at getting on for $2 billion. Yes, C$ is smaller in size than US$, but then so is the economy and the whole financial market. 0.1% management fee is not the lowest of the low (some index funds are down at 0.07%), but if we think of the rollover paperwork, such an ETF might save us, it's quite possibly wholly worth it.

As an open-ended fund, CBIL is always going to trade at or at least around net asset value (it's currently off by 1 cent on $50 and change, which is, c'm on, nothing) and that's about it really.

Why I'm wrong

Well, there could be non-Canadians who are interested in a C$ income. Other than that, I've not really said anything that is disputable. This is a short-term government bond fund. Which therefore provides a low-ish income along with capital value (nominal, obviously), maintenance, and speedy access given liquidity.

My opinion

As with the slightly differently constructed USFR in the US market:

It's a fine instrument that meets a reasonable need. It's not something to trade, it's something to - maybe and perhaps - have a few months' worth of living expenses in while the rest of an asset base is more aggressively invested.

Nothing wrong with it at all, and quite a lot right. It's just not at that exciting end of an investment strategy.

Juicing our returns on our near-cash savings by moving into this ETF is sensible. Boring but sensible.

That really is the opinion here, too. We always make trade-off risk against income. Savings risk comes as both credit risk and interest rate risk, both of which are minimized here. That does mean the income is less than exciting. But on the other hand, our savings portfolio (as opposed to, as at the top, our investment portfolio) should include some near immediate access to cash while earning what we can, given that insistence upon capital and credit safety and near immediate access.

CBIL:CA handles all of this just fine. That's what the construction is, that's what it's for, and it works at doing it for that portion of our savings that we want to subject to such conditions.

data.title: Palo Alto Earnings Preview: New Kid On The Block Is AI & I'm Buying
data.description:
Earnings preview:

Palo Alto Netwroks (NASDAQ:PANW) is set to report 3Q25 on May 20th. The company expects NGS ARR in the range of $5.03 billion- $5.08 billion, an increase of 33%- 34% year over year, while RPO is expected at $13.5 billion to $13.6 billion, up 19% to 20% year over year. Revenue guidance is in the range of $2.26 billion to $2.29 billion, an increase of 14%- 15% is in line with estimates of $2.27 billion, representing growth of 14.6%, while company expects EPS in the range of $0.76 to $0.77, up 15%- 17%, against consensus at $0.77, representing a year over year change of over 16.7%.

I last wrote on Palo Alto in late August of last year after the company reported 4Q24. The stock was trading at around $172 at the time, and is now up around 11.5% since, outperforming the S&P500, up around 5% during the same period. I had expected ARR growth from Palo Alto’s platformization progress, and share gains in the XDR market due to the company’s integration of AI and shaky consumer confidence in rival Crowdstrike (CRWD) after last July’s global IT outage. I’m updating my thesis and maintaining the stock with a buy pre-3Q25 earnings, as I see a possible upward revision of FY25 guidance, with the tough year-over-year comparisons now behind them and macro headwinds easing. I think management has room to lift guidance, and I think the market would react positively to that. Buying a stock pre-earnings is a risky move, more so in this market, because investors are panicking, and post-earnings stock price performance is not moving on fundamentals.

Investment thesis:

While I’m bullish on the company for the long term, I think there could be some upside ahead in the short term on 1. Customer refresh cycles, and 2. Grabbing market share from competitors. The company used financing back in 2023 and 2024, which could show a muted recovery, but the business remains cyclical, as is the case with Fortinet (FTNT). Palo Alto’s firewall refresh cycle is predictable and well documented; customers typically replace firewalls every 5.5 to 7 years due to aging hardware and security concerns, meaning the company can anticipate when older sales will convert into fresh demand. So if $800 million in firewalls were sold six years ago, a similar amount is now due for renewal. For the longer term, I’m optimistic about the following:

  • The company’s acquisition of AI Protect, an industry leader in securing the use of AI and ML applications and models. By integrating its capabilities, Palo Alto expands its coverage into AI model security, which is currently projected to become more and more critical as AI adoption accelerates. The acquisition supports the company’s broader strategy, as it did with Prisma Cloud, to build out a platform via strategic M&A. So, combined with the launch of Prisma AIRS, a new AI security platform, the company is one of the first vendors offering an end-to-end AI security platform that allows organizations to “deploy AI bravely by protecting the entire AI development lifecycle to meet enterprise requirements for model scanning, risk assessment, GenAI runtime security, posture management, and AI agent security.” I think the acquisition gives Palo Alto a first-mover advantage in a high-growth segment, potentially creating a new revenue stream and deepening its presence in large enterprise and government accounts that are heavily investing in AI. The deal is set to close 1Q26, and according to Globes, the purchase price will be around $650 million to $700 million. I think we’ll be seeing more of such acquisitions in the future simply because the strategy seems to be working.

  • Management’s focus is shifting from hardware to software. As of last quarter, both hardware and software were growing 21%; the real growth engine was software, while hardware was dragging the average down. According to CEO Nikesh Arora, the company has been

transitioning our network security business, as you saw from harder-to-software. This is why you see that we're growing that category 21% between hardware and software. So that just over time reduces our reliance on hardware because I think that cloud transformation is underway.

It’s notable to say that cloud volume is going up faster than data center volumes are declining, and I think the shift from physical products to cloud and software will lead to better long-term growth and profitability. Cloud security remains one of the fastest-growing segments within security, expected to grow at a CAGR of 23.5% between 2024 and 2029 to $25.9 billion in 2029, from $8.9 billion in 2024, according to Gartner. I think the company is well-positioned to lead the next phase of cloud security as the market evolves from fixing cloud misconfigurations (CSPM) to securing software code and now protecting real-time production environments: the company's Cortex Cloud platform unifies all these layers into a single stack. And on top of that, the transition to SASE last quarter showed that customer value increased 2.5x due to the reduced need for physical appliances, manual upgrades, and service costs. Palo Alto is the only vendor offering a unified network security platform that has “a hardware, software and SASE form factor, which runs on a single platform. To replicate us, you'd have to at least have 3 to 5 vendors to replicate a network security platform,” said Arora last March in a Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference. Around 7 years ago, Palo Alto wasn’t a player in the SASE market, but the SASE business is now “almost as big as our hardware business.”

For all these reasons and more, I like the company on a fundamental level, and see more upside for the stock in FY26 as a result. The company is leveraging AI and wants a place at the table. Palo Alto also announced Prisma Access Browser 2.0 and Cortex XSIAM 3.0, further bolstering its aim to lead in AI security.

Valuation and price movement:

The stock isn’t cheap at current levels, but this isn’t the buy Palo Alto dirt cheap kind of buy. The stock is in the green year to date and on the one month chart, up 6% and 13% respectively and recovering from the lows of $152 per share back in April, up around 26% since then, I don’t think this is a buy on the dip opportunity, but I think long-term investors are better positioned to add around current levels before the stock soars to its 52-week high of around $208 per share.

Palo Alto has a forward PE ratio of 63.8x, below Crowdstrike at 127.4x and CyberArk (CYBR) at 110.8x. The company also has EV/Sales ratio of 13.5x, in line with CyberArk’s at 12.9x and lower than CrowdStrike’s at 22x.

The stock is currently trading above all its EMAs, with EMA21 at $183.6, EMA50 at $180.9, and EMA200 at $177.4. The stock shows the bullish trend is still intact, with RSI at 61, recovering from oversold levels back in early April. A downside risk is always there, though, and it looks like a short-term pullback is forming, as is seen in the last few candles. With earnings coming up, some investors could be counting profits, which is creating short-term pressure. I think if the company’s guidance doesn’t either beat or raise, the stock could shoot up as expectations are high. Support levels are currently at the EMA21 and EMA50, while resistance currently stands at $196, a recent high. I would recommend investors wait for a further pullback. With the stock down around 0.5% on Wednesday, you could still get in on a lower price.

All in all, I’m optimistic about the company in the long term and see short-term pullback as an opportunity, not an indication of fundamental weakness.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Tariff Truce Made This Dividend King Less Timely
data.description:
XOM stock: FQ1 earnings report and tariff rates

I last analyzed Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) stock in March 2025. In an article entitled "Exxon Mobil Is A Better Bet Than XLE On Oil Any Price Recovery”, I rated the stock as a strong buy. My key consideration was that XOM is better positioned than the sector to benefit from an oil price recovery.

Since then, there have been a few important new catalysts evolving both around XOM itself and the macroscopic geopolitics. In the former bucket, XOM just released its FQ1 earnings report (ER) early in May and described a few notable updates. In the late bucket, the tariff disputes started on the so-called "Liberation Day" (i.e., April 2) have been a dominating – and still evolving – factor for the overall equity market. When the tariff roller-coaster just started in early April, I think XOM is a very good energy stock to own as the extreme uncertainty roiled markets. As seen in the next chart, it has indeed suffered relatively lower drawdowns in April due to its steward characteristics in the energy sector.

These uncertainties have gradually subsided since then. Since the Liberation Day, the U.S. has extended a 90-day grace period to most of its trading patterns and also reached an agreement with UK. The latest development – also the most important one – is the 90 day truce agreed by U.S. and China early this week as detailed below.

Seeking Alpha News: The U.S. and China have agreed to a significant reduction in tariffs on each other’s goods for 90 days. Each country will cut tariffs by 115%, with the U.S. cutting tariffs on Chinese imports from 145% to 30%, and China cutting tariffs on U.S. imports from 125% to 10%. The tariff reductions are set for an initial period of 90 days, after which further negotiations are expected.

With the uncertainties dissipating, XOM began to lag the overall market starting late April as you can see from the figure above. These macroscopic developments, combined with the business updates provided in its FQ1 ER as to be detailed next, have weakened XOM’s timeliness compared to the time of my last writing and let me adjust its rating to buy.

XOM stock: earnings growth outlook

XOM announced its latest earnings for FQ1 of 2025 on May 2, 2025, with a set of uneven results in my view. The Normalized EPS and GAAP EPS both came in at $1.76, beating estimates by a small margin of $0.02. However, total revenue of $83.13 billion missed the estimate by a good gap of $2.96 billion. Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter, expected on August 1, 2025 (Pre-Market), the EPS Normalized Estimate is $1.51, and the Revenue Estimate is $80.40 billion, translating into sequential decline of about 15% and 3%, respectively.

Looking further ahead, the decline is expected to extend to the full fiscal 2025. For the full FY 2025, the EPS estimate is $6.59, representing a year-over-year decrease of -15.36%. The EPS estimate points to a recovery in FY 2026 to $7.82. The recovery is expected to continue for the next few years and the compounded annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) for EPS between 2025 and December 2027 is estimated at 13.9%.

I think the above estimate is too optimistic. My method for estimating long-term growth rates is described in other articles. The end results are quoted below:

The method involves the return on capital employed (“ROCE”) and the reinvestment rate (“RR”). The ROCE for XOM has been around 26.4% in recent years as seen in the chart below. Assuming an RR of about 40% in the next few years, XOM organic growth rate would be ~ 10.56% (which equals 26.4% ROCE x 40% RR).

The 40% RR is estimated from its CAPEX budget announced in the ER, which was in the range of $27-$29 B for FY 2025. Next, I will discuss the implications on its return potential.

XOM stock: valuation

Given XOM’s status as a dividend king, it is a fitting place to apply the discounted dividend model (“DDM”) to assess its fair valuation. To be more explicit, I will use the following 2-stage DDM as detailed in my earlier article:

There are a total of 3 key parameters in the 2-stage DDM: the discount rate, the growth rate in stage 1, and the terminal growth rate. For the discount rate, I relied on the so-called WACC, the weighted average cost of the capital model. The discount rate for XOM is about 9.7% on average in recent years following this model.

For the growth rate in the 1st stage, I will use the 10.56% I estimated above for the next 10 years. However, I do not expect a 40% reinvest indefinitely. Thus, as an estimating of its terminal growth rate, I assumed a reinvestment rate of 20%, which is about the sector average. Under this assumption, XOM’s perpetual growth rate would be ~5.28% (which equals 26.4% ROCE x 20% RR). Plugging in all 3 inputs, the table below shows the final results from the 2-stage DDM with its FWD dividend of $3.96 (i.e., quarterly payout of $0.99 per share). As seen, XOM’s fair price is around $139, compared to its stock price of $109 as of this writing. As such, the stock still leaves a good margin of safety.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few downside risks are worth mentioning. The current Brent crude oil prices, hovering around $60, are quite low and present a challenging pricing environment for XOM. It is uncertain how the trade disputes will eventually play out, which can impact export/import dynamics and also oil prices. Furthermore, the production decisions from OPEC are also unpredictable and can impact oil prices. In addition, XOM often face regulation risks and lawsuits as our society emphasis energy and environmental sustainability. As a latest example, the Colorado Supreme Court recently announced its decision to let Boulder to sue Exxon (and also Suncor) over climate change. These risks can cause both negative financial impacts and also reputation losses for XOM.

To conclude, my experiences have taught me that business operations determine long-term stock price trend, while near-term price behavior is dominated by market sentiment. That is why our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) monitors catalysts on both fronts to delineate risks and maximum gain. For XOM, we keep seeing a strong business for sale at a sizable discount judging by the updates provided in its FQ1 ER. However, the changes in macroscopic geopolitics since my last writing have weakened its timeliness and promoted me to adjust its rating to buy.

data.title: Nintendo's Imminent Switch 2 Launch
data.description:
Nintendo Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:NTDOF) (OTCPK:NTDOY) reported FY 2025 earnings on Thursday last week. I don't think this year's earnings are relevant, but before jumping into what's important (guidance and Switch 2 commentary), let's take a quick look at the numbers. As expected, both sales and profits dropped considerably in FY 2025:

The underlying reason for this drop is straightforward: the Switch 1 is in its eighth year and Nintendo has not launched many strong 1p titles lately (which makes sense considering the imminent arrival of the Switch 2). We also must consider that Nintendo first mentioned the Switch 2 on January 16th (even though the Direct was hosted on April 2nd) and that this announcement might have significantly reduced sales in Q4. The Switch 2 announcement likely impacted Q4 sales (when it was officially announced) and the entire year, as pretty much everyone was expecting an announcement by Nintendo. Despite this, Nintendo sold 10.8 million Switch 1 in FY 2025 (short of the 11 million Q3 forecast) and enjoyed a strong holiday season:

As usual, management was more optimistic about hardware sales than software sales. Software reached 155 million, almost 4% above the guidance set in Q3. I've discussed this before, but it's a regular pattern at Nintendo. It's great for shareholders because software carries a significantly higher margin, and therefore Nintendo tends to be more pessimistic with its earnings than its sales guidance.

Margins are an interesting topic when looking at the Switch 1 and the Switch 2 guidance and how margins might develop throughout the lifecycle of the latter. Nintendo's gross margin actually expanded by 390 basis points despite the drop in sales.

The reason? The higher proportion of software sales as hardware units falls faster than software sales. Nintendo makes a good chunk of its software profits in software, so if these weigh more in overall sales, then it's relatively normal to see margins expand. This is a good framework when thinking about FY 2026 guidance and the lifecycle of the Switch 2 in general, but I'll discuss that later.

Operating margins dropped significantly despite the gross margin expansion. The main reason for this contraction is that Nintendo continues to invest ahead of the SW2 launch. This ultimately means that current investments are not met with sales from the SW2 (something that will change over time). From this year's numbers, I would also highlight the first drop in a while in annual playing users:

Not extremely surprising considering the lack of 1P blockbusters and the announcement of the SW2. The launch of the SW2 should help reinvigorate this metric (especially thanks to all of its collaborative features), but one of the good things about the launch of the SW2 is that it builds off this existing user base. We shouldn't expect to see it grow at the same pace it did for Switch 1, but at the same time, it will start from a significantly larger base.

After taking a quick look at the (mostly irrelevant) yearly numbers, let's take a look at what everyone was focused on: guidance.

Nintendo's SW2 Guidance

Nintendo released a two-part guidance, one with the financial figures and another with hardware and software units of the different platforms (as it typically does). The financial guidance portrayed a significant rise in sales (+63%) coupled with a significantly lower rise in operating profit (+13%):

This margin contraction might have worried some people, but it requires context and I believe there is a significant upside opportunity. First, it's inevitable for margins to come down when a new system is launched. The reason is the opposite of what I discussed earlier. During a new platform's early years, revenue will be more tilted toward hardware sales, which carry a significantly lower margin than software. Nintendo even mentioned that SW2 gross margins are lower than those of the SW1, putting to rest some concerns around price gouging its customers. Seeing a price hike definitely hurts, but let's not forget that Nintendo had been 9 years without raising prices!

As the installed base expands and more titles are launched, margins should naturally expand as software becomes a bigger portion of the pie. In short, it's natural to see an immediate margin reset at system launch.

This said, I believe there's upside opportunity for profits here (will delve into sales later), for two reasons. First, Nintendo has a history of being conservative with software sales. This means that software might become a larger portion of the pie even at launch (TBD).

The second reason is that management assumes tariffs stay where they are. This basically means that any positive developments between Vietnam/China and the U.S. could potentially result in the tariff amount accruing to Nintendo (we already saw positive developments this week regarding China). Worth noting that Nintendo is not currently suffering the retaliatory tariffs (there was a pause in these), but it is facing the 10% flat rate. I don't think tariffs are ultimately as relevant for Nintendo due to most profits coming from the software side, but it's definitely a potential upside opportunity.

Nintendo expects to sell 15 million SW2 in FY 2026 (10 months of sales due to the system being launched two months into the year) and 45 million software units:

The software units don't include the bundled software sold with the hardware, so if we assume that 50% of hardware units will be sold with Mario Kart World (which will arguably be higher), this means that software sales would be somewhere around 52 million for a 3.46 tie ratio. Management argued this was a good benchmark to think about.

This seems a tad conservative when considering that the tie ratio for the SW1 was higher (3.57) despite having significantly less support from third-party publishers. As discussed above, Nintendo tends to be quite conservative with software sales, and this calculation seems to support this belief somewhat.

Hardware also seems conservative if we consider several things. First, management also tends to be conservative with hardware sales at launch. They projected around 10 million in sales for the SW1 at launch, and sold close to 18 million over the first 12 months. Management openly shared that the current figure assumes that the SW2 doesn't sell faster than the SW1 (something I assumed in my valuation exercise), but I would say there's upside to this stemming from two things:

  1. The SW platform is proven now and people know what to expect

  2. Pre-order demand is significantly outpacing that of the SW1 and also management's expectations.

There were a lot of doubts regarding Nintendo's capacity to supply demand. A supply constraint could theoretically limit the upside if demand ends up being higher than expected. Management quickly put this fear to rest, claiming that the forecast was based on demand and not supply:

The limits of our hardware production capacity were not a factor in setting our sales volume forecast.

This is consistent with the fact that they managed to manufacture 18 million SW1 in their first year and that they prepared for a bigger launch. I struggle to see 15 million as the peak supply for Nintendo. What seems evident is that Nintendo can't sell 30 million SW2 in its first year, even if there's demand for it. The good news is that this shouldn't matter much. Whether Nintendo sells in its first or second year doesn't change much of the investment thesis.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dropbox: Losing Hope As Customer Counts Keep Bleeding
data.description:
I view 2025 as a sea change type of year in both the stock markets and the economy, as tariffs ripple through industries in a disproportionate manner and AI revolutionizes the corporate workforce. In such a year, the gap between winners and losers is going to widen: and so amid a recovering stock market, investors should not get complacent and continue to emphasize careful stock picking.

Dropbox (NASDAQ:DBX), in my view, has been showcasing losing trends for the past couple of years. The file sync and share company rallied after reporting Q1 results, bringing year-to-date gains to roughly flat and in line with the S&P 500 despite tremendous fundamental headwinds facing this name.

I last wrote a bearish opinion on Dropbox in March, when the stock was trading at slightly more modest prices near $26. Since then, Dropbox has rallied on the most minor of guidance boosts to the full year, lifting only the low end of its recent range by one point of growth. At the same time, the company has shed paying customers for two consecutive quarters. With that in mind, I’m reiterating my sell rating on this stock.

One of the things that investors have to be wary of here is how quickly Dropbox is losing relevance and credibility in a shifting IT landscape, driven by AI. Like many software companies, Dropbox has also put in its “me too” attempt at an AI product in the form of Dropbox Dash. In part fueled by hopes for Dash, Dropbox claims that its TAM is growing at a 27% CAGR to hit $21.6 billion by FY28, which would mean Dropbox is only ~1% penetrated into this overall market.

The problem is that Dropbox’s purported AI features may have been rebranded but offer nothing truly revolutionary. Dash is effectively a search feature, allowing users to find stored content while also offering cookie-cutter summarization and auto-write capabilities that free ChatGPT can offer. Dropbox is also behind Box (BOX) in terms of enterprise search, whose Box Skills product has been able to index and search content for several years.

We need to look no further than Dropbox’s revenue decline and customer count decline to cement the notion that Dropbox Dash, and AI itself, are not incremental drivers for Dropbox.

Dropbox isn’t a growth stock, in other words. Now, what Dropbox IS, at least for the time being, is a stable cash flow generator. Multiple rounds of layoffs and an effort to shrink its real estate footprint have shown that Dropbox is serious about maintaining its cash flow (which it has redirected into buybacks). Amid tepid growth, Dropbox has affirmed its FCF guidance for the year for “at least $950 million,” representing a 38% FCF margin that’s higher than the $859 million of FCF that Dropbox has generated over the trailing twelve months.

Dropbox is also relatively modestly valued against that FCF profile. At current share prices near $30, Dropbox trades at an $8.10 billion market cap. After we net off the $1.18 billion of cash and $2.35 billion of term loans and convertible debt on Dropbox’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $9.27 billion.

This puts Dropbox’s valuation at just 9.7x EV/FY25 FCF against the minimum of the company’s guidance for the year. Here’s the issue, however: while I have high confidence in Dropbox’s ability to hit near-term FCF goals, the longer term is shakier.

Dropbox is a software company that is no longer growing. Not only is that an indicator of dis-economies of scale at risk in the future, but it also means that Dropbox is losing in the technology shift toward AI. Rival Box (BOX), which has long touted superior enterprise grade capabilities to Dropbox’s more consumer oriented platform, achieved high single digit revenue growth in the same quarter that Dropbox declined, indicating that Dropbox is losing market share and maybe more importantly, mindshare as well as IT leaders look to companies with a better innovation profile.

All in all, I think Dropbox will hold value in the near term as it keeps pumping out cash flow to fuel buybacks, but it won’t be long until revenue declines eventually make this cash flow king bleed. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q1 Download

Let’s now go through Dropbox’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Dropbox’s revenue declined -1% y/y to $624.7 million, though this slightly beat Wall Street’s expectations of $619.8 million (-2% y/y) by a one-point margin.

One of my bigger concerns, however, has been the fact that Dropbox has continued to shed paying customers. As shown in the chart below, paid customers dropped sequentially for the second consecutive quarter, down to 18.16 million payers and down ~60k versus Q4.

To me, this showcases that AI actually may be harming Dropbox in two ways. One is via migration of deals toward better-equipped enterprise rivals like Box. The second and potentially more overlooked driver is the fact that AI is forcing many companies to rethink their headcount structures and, in many cases, lay off or freeze hiring in favor of implementing automation. Dropbox still prices its products on a per-user basis (though there is an element of data usage in its packages as most of the smaller-business tiers have limited pooled storage, and businesses have to add on extra after utilizing this capacity), and so corporate headcount trimming has a direct impact on its ARR.

In the past, Dropbox was able to muscle past slower user growth by emphasizing upsells into higher-priced subscription tiers (Plus and Professional plans for individuals and Advanced packages for businesses). However, this lever has stalled as well. ARPU (average revenue per user) fell slightly on both a sequential and y/y basis to $139.26.

Thankfully, operating margins are still making meaningful leaps. In Q1, Dropbox’s margin improved to 41.7%, a major 520bps y/y leap versus 36.5% in the year-ago quarter.

The margin gains, meanwhile, were driven by the company’s headcount slimming last year as well as its strategic decision to pull back on marketing for its dwindling FormSwift subsidiary. Still, cost cuts can only go so far; ultimately, a software company relies on high gross margins and economies of scale to drive profit growth, the latter of which Dropbox no longer possesses with its declining client count.

Key Takeaways

To me, Dropbox remains a classic value trap. Its current free cash flow profile and single-digit FCF multiples are very appealing, until we pause to consider the fact that the company’s earnings power will eventually be hamstrung by its declining customer base.

I’d be willing to explore a buy or at least neutral opinion on Dropbox when the company proves it can stabilize its customer counts back to flat; until then, remain on the sidelines.

data.title: Plug Power: Sell On Likely Loss Of Hydrogen Production Tax Credit (Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Plug Power Inc. or “Plug Power” (NASDAQ:PLUG) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

After the close of Monday's session, ailing hydrogen fuel cell product solutions provider Plug Power reported Q1 2025 results in line with the company's pre-announcement two weeks ago. On a year-over-year basis, net sales increased by approximately 11% to $133.7 million:

While sales of fuel cell systems and hydrogen infrastructure took another hit, substantial price increases for the company's maintenance and fueling services resulted in core material handling revenues growing by 13.5%:

Sales in the much-touted electrolyzer segment continued to be lumpy. The business has been underperforming expectations since inception and resulted in consolidated product gross margin turning negative in recent years:

However, recent price increases in combination with the company's cost reduction efforts have resulted in lower gross losses across all segments in Q1.

Adjusted for the accounting impact of warrants issued to key customers Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT), consolidated gross margin improved from a record low of (123.9%) in Q1 2024 to (45.3%):

During the questions-and-answers sessions of the conference call, management backpedaled on previously stated targets of gross margins turning positive in Q4 2025 and now aims for breakeven levels. However, even this appears to be an overly ambitious target, as reducing costs even further might be a challenging endeavor.

Please note also that service gross margin has been boosted by substantial releases from previously established reserves for losses on unfavorable service contracts. Adjusted for the releases, service gross margin remains in deeply negative territory:

While down by almost 50% on a year-over-year basis, cash burn remained significant:

However, persistent shareholder dilution helped the company replenishing its dwindling cash balances and actually add some much needed liquidity.

As a result, cash and cash equivalents increased from $205.7 million at the end of 2024 to $295.8 million.

Subsequent to quarter-end, Plug Power entered into an expensive, up to $525 million secured debt facility with existing lender Yorkville Advisors:

As a result, the company expects to abstain from further equity issuances this year.

On the conference call, management admitted to regulatory challenges in the domestic market, with House Republicans seeking to axe the all-important U.S. clean hydrogen production tax credit. The proposal was approved by the House Ways and Means Committee on Tuesday and will now head to the House floor.

Should the tax credits indeed expire by the end of this year, the company would be required to start construction of its planned Texas green hydrogen facility over the next couple of quarters but given the adverse regulatory framework, the Department of Energy is even less likely to fund the up to $1.66 billion loan guarantee approved under the Biden administration.

Without additional green hydrogen plants and related production tax credits, the company's hydrogen fueling business will likely continue to generate substantial gross losses for years to come.

A lack of tax credits would also put an end to most third-party green hydrogen projects in the United States, thus further limiting the company's ability to sell electrolyzers and liquefaction equipment domestically.

As a result of negative developments in the core U.S. market, management is now focusing on Europe with an alleged "electrolyzer opportunity funnel worth over $21 billion across 2025 and 2026" and expectations for the region "to be a multi gigawatt contributor to bookings and revenue over the next 18 to 24 months with meaningful margin contribution."

However, the company has been present in the European market for many years already with very limited success due to a combination of strong domestic competition and poor execution.

In addition, the company's efforts to enter the European FCEV market through a joint venture with Renault have been a total failure, with the HYVIA joint venture currently in liquidation after filing for bankruptcy in late 2024.

Adding insult to injury, Plug Power's sole announced project under its joint venture with Acciona Energy in Spain has not even started construction, despite the website still stating that green hydrogen production is expected to commence "in early 2025."

Other planned large-scale deployments like the 100 MW green hydrogen project with Galp at the Sines Refinery in Portugal have also experienced delays.

As a result of the trade war initiated by the Trump administration, European customers will likely be even less eager to buy from a U.S.-based company going forward.

Consequently, Plug Power would have to aggressively compete on price again or move electrolyzer stack production to Europe, but the company does not have the funds to do so.

Moreover, with the stock price hovering near multi-year lows, raising additional equity would be heavily dilutive and could quickly turn into a death spiral, as experienced by zero emission truck manufacturer and former strategic partner Nikola Corporation (OTC:NKLAQ) over the past year:

While Plug Power appears to have sufficient liquidity for the remainder of the year, 2026 might be a very different story, and with secured lender Yorkville Advisors now sitting at the top of the capital structure, common shareholders would likely end up holding the bag in bankruptcy, as usual.

To avoid Nikola Corporation's fate, management would be well-served to reduce cash usage further. But even when assuming gross margins to reach breakeven levels by the end of the year, the company would still be saddled with hundreds of millions of dollars in annual operating expenses, debt service requirements and capital expenditures.

That said, some of these expenses might be covered by persistent releases of cash collateral as legacy sale-and-leaseback agreements move closer to their scheduled end.

But without the hydrogen production tax credit, the company will struggle to improve fueling margins as investments in additional green hydrogen plants don't make sense anymore.

Challenging market conditions are very much evidenced by an approximately 30% decline in backlog over the past 18 months:

Please note that more than 60% of the company's backlog is related to multi-year services, fueling and leasing agreements.

With limited backlog available for recognition this year, 2025 revenues won't be anywhere close to Plug Power's original guidance range of $850 million to $950 million provided at the symposium in November:

However, management already provided a hint to the company's true 2025 revenue performance during the questions-and-answers session of the Q4 conference call in March when the CFO projected Q1 sales to be approximately 15% to 20% of full-year revenues.

As a result, analysts have reduced expectations across the board with the current consensus now sitting at just $730 million. That said, this would still represent more than 15% year-over-year growth following an abysmal 2024 performance.

With the company's business plan now in jeopardy and limited funding options beyond this year, I am downgrading Plug Power's shares from "Hold" to "Sell."

Bottom Line

Plug Power reported improved Q1 2025 results due to a combination of price increases for the company's service offerings and aggressive cost reduction efforts.

However, gross margins remained in deeply negative territory and the business continues to consume substantial amounts of cash.

While management is targeting breakeven gross margins by the end of the year, Plug Power would still have to cover several hundred million of cash operating expenses, debt service obligations and capital expenditures.

Even more importantly, the proposed expiration of the hydrogen production tax credit by the end of the year would heavily impact the company's business plan, which is based on constructing a domestic network of green hydrogen plants with funds provided by the Department of Energy.

While Plug Power appears to have sufficient liquidity for the remainder of the year, the company's low share price will limit its ability to conduct additional equity offerings without massive dilution for common shareholders. Similar to former strategic partner Nikola Corporation last year, Plug Power might end up in a death spiral with funds raised from equity offerings no longer sufficient to cover persistent cash burn.

With secured lender Yorkville Advisors now sitting at the very top of the company's capital structure, common shareholders are likely to hold the bag in a potential bankruptcy.

With the company's business plan now in jeopardy and limited funding options beyond this year, I am downgrading Plug Power's shares from "Hold" to "Sell."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Investing Outlook For China As U.S. Trade Tensions Ease
data.description:
Trade tensions between China and the U.S. have eased after the two counties agreed to slash tariffs for 90 days. Haining Zha, VP & Director, Asset Allocation Research, TD Asset Management discusses what the de-escalation in trade tensions means for investors.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie - Well, China and the United States have announced an easing of trade tensions, with both countries lowering their tariff barriers. Joining us now to discuss what this means for China's economy and markets is Haining Zha, Vice Present and Director for Asset Allocation Research with TD Asset Management. Haining, thanks for joining the show.

Haining Zha - Thanks for having me.

Anthony Okolie - All right. So as mentioned, we have a reduction of tariffs in both China and the US. What's your reaction to this development?

Haining Zha - It's certainly very positive, both for the market and for the world economy. And it is positive, both in terms of headline, as well as underlying details. So in terms of headline, drop of tariff rate from 145% down to 30%-- that is a huge drop. So that is certainly exceeding our expectations. So before, we were thinking about it is probably somewhere around 40%. And that is after long and hard negotiations.

And just after one meeting, we're already down to 30%, which is very encouraging. And in terms of underlying details, there are actually a lot. For example, in the official statement, there is no mention of purchase of energy and agricultural products, no commitment on further purchase of Boeing airplanes. So all of these details can come up later, which means there could continue to produce positive outcome.

And also, fentanyl, that is another bright spot. So from China's side, the Minister of Public Security actually joined the meeting. And there seems to be some surprisingly positive progress there. And as we know, the fentanyl-related tariff is 20%. So there is definitely room for cuts on that front, as well.

Anthony Okolie - OK, so there's definitely some optimism. And the US has characterized this as a reset in relations. And President Trump says he actually may speak with the Chinese President later this week. What will you be watching going forward?

Haining Zha - Well, people talk about a reset. But actually, we can look at it from different angles. On one hand, if we look at the near term, it is, indeed, a very positive reset. Essentially, we are going from an escalation stage, going to a de-escalation stage, which is certainly positive. But on the other hand, I think what is not changing is the great struggle between those superpowers is not ending.

So we have to be very kind of sober and prepare for the future volatility. And in terms of what we are watching for, I think number one, fentanyl, as I mentioned, because 20% is a lot. It could easily be cut in half or completely be removed, which is certainly positive for the market. And we also have to watch out for what are the potential items that will go into the negotiation agenda.

For example, are China further opening up? Are there any concrete measures on that front that it will be worth watching? A third interesting area is, as we know, US don't want a general decoupling. But they are choosing selective decoupling in strategic industries, such as AI and semiconductors. On those fronts, it will be interesting to see how much decoupling will there be and if it will cause supply chain disruptions.

Anthony Okolie - Yeah, there's still a lot of details that need to be worked out. How has China's economy managed through this recent trade turmoil? And how much of a boost could this tariff reprieve provide to China?

Haining Zha - Right. So far, I think that China managed its economy in the first quarter really well, partly due to two reasons. Number one, it's the pull-forward effect, stemming from the tariff. The second is the organic recovery of the Chinese economy. So in terms of that pull-forward effect-- actually, let's look at the overall Q1 Chinese economy performance.

In Q1, the GDP is growing at 5.4% above the full year target of 5%. The retail consumption is a tad better than last year. The infrastructure investment is also growing at a robust pace, around 5%. So the only area that is dragging is the real estate investment-- still down percent year over year. And in terms of export volume, it is actually still growing.

So for example, in April, the export to the US dropped 21%. But the overall export volume still growing about 8%, which is quite amazing. But the full tariff impact is not completely reflected in the April number, given that all of the super high tariff happened at the latter half of the month. So in May, that impact might be a little bit bigger.

But in the meantime, the policy support is on the way. For example, a couple of days ago, we are seeing in terms of monetary policy, the PBOC is cutting benchmark interest rate by 10 basis points, cutting Reserve requirement ratio by 50 basis points. So all of these support are on the way.

Anthony Okolie - So there's additional government stimulus coming from China?

Haining Zha - Yeah.

Anthony Okolie - OK. Amid this backdrop, how should investors be thinking about China's markets right now?

Haining Zha - Yeah, we have been pretty constructive about the Chinese equity market since the start of the year-- not because of very low valuation but because of the innovation that we observed from the bottom-up company level and also the competitiveness of their products. Tariff is certainly a headwind, but that tariff headwind is also abating.

And I know for a lot of institutional and retail investors, China is kind out of benchmark play. It will take a lot for people to add China exposure. And they really have to understand it really well to be comfortable with that. But I think even for investor investing in a developed market, it will be worth watching the Chinese economy and markets. Because many of these innovations, it will have huge spillover effect to the rest of the world. And by watching it, monitoring it closely, it will position the investor better for what's coming.

Original Post
